Überblick," Gnomon 57 (1985) 127-34.
The bimillenary fever has abated somewhat but the fall-out from 1981 and
1982 continues to appear in belated publications or delayed arrivals. Antonie
Wlosok's collected titles and notices is exemplary and enormously useful for those
who wish to assess recent trends in Vergilian scholarship and to discover centers of
concentration.

A. Köhnken, "Sola. . .tua carmina (Vergil, Ecl . 8,9f)," WüJbb NF 10
(1984) 77-90.
Gazich and Hering are both concerned with Eclogue 1; Hering's review of
Eclogue 1 appears in pp. 184-207. Van Sickle provides valuable instruction in his
"reading" of the first Eclogue , and contests Ian DuQuesnay's strictures in Papers ,
Liverpool Latin Seminar 3 (1981) 29-182. The troublesome riddles of Damoetas (E.
3, 104f) and Menalcas (106f) are studied by Hofmann with reference to Aratus'
Phainomena. Beaujeu despairs of an identification for the Wunderkind and settles for
a new generation, an anonymous child of the Roman aristocracy. Bevilacqua recognizes divination and possibly Hebraic influence in the Messianic Eclogue but stresses
the Greco- Roman traits of history, work, and peace. Monteleone favors Lactantius
(Div. inst. 7, 24, 9-11) and pre-fifth century writers who follow a text providing:
18-20, 23, 26-7, 31-41, 28, 30, 21-22, and 24-5. The net result is a sophisticated
genethliakon rather than prophecy. Knox examines the "intensely Callimachean"
setting of the Sixth Eclogue and provides a parallel for Gallus' initiation in the antiCallimachean, Antimachus of Colophon.

Ella Schwartz, Aspects of Orpheus in Classical Literature and Mythology.
Diss. Harvard 1984. Abstract: HSCP 89 (1985) 240-3.
Tandy offers a fresh assessment of ingredere ( G . 1.42): "enter (your
temple)," or "cross the threshold." Harbinson detects the short-toed eagle (Circaetus
gallicus) rather than the white stork (ciconia) in Vergil's white bird (Č7. 2.319-20).
Landolfi treats Vergil's negative view of love and in particular the sphragis of his
attitude in the celebrated excursus ( G . 3.242-83). Makowski detects playful wit in
the juxtaposition of Maecenas with haud mollia iussa , thereby accenting a salient
feature of his patron's persona. Abramowicz regards the Aristaeus epyllion as a
replacement for the eulogy of Gallus which was censored by Augustus. Schwartz,
inter alia , associates G. 4 and Tibullus 1.7 and underscores their pervasive concern
with poetry's ability to confer immortality.

R. D. Williams, The Aeneid of Virgil. A Companion to the Transl
C. Day Lewis (with introduction, commentary, and glossary). (Bristol 1985
Cairns examines the poles of Discord and Concord around which the epic
moves. The Aeneiďs first simile (1.148-54) Cairns associates with Marc Antony's
discord. Cattiverà underlines Vergil's concern for Italy, united and consolidated by
Rome. Cleary's review article continues to offer useful counsel to high-school
teachers. Colucci examines the movement of stars in A. 2.9; 2.250; 6.539; and 8.369.
Segal's translation includes the larger part of Conte's two earlier volumes, both well
received: Memoria dei poeti e sistema letterario (1974) and II genere e i suoi confini
(1980, 1984). Particularly valuable are Conte's interpretation of Eclogue 10,
Aristaeus, Orpheus and the Georgics , Vergil's Aeneid , the Baldric of Pallas, and the
Helen Episode. Dauge argues for astronomical symbolism in the 12 books of the
epic, both solar and stellar. Hardie's new volume explores cosmology and
imperialism in the epic. Harrison's prodigies include the horse's head (1, 443-9) and
the Table Eating prophecy. Hauck's dissertation deals with the temple wall decorations at Carthage, and the temple doors at Cumae, and accents Vergil's originality
and psychological insights in the descriptions. Jenkyns argues forcefully for a more
detached view of Vergilian pessimism and melancholy; a thoughtful, contentious and
spirited plea for reassessment of contemporary views which so often abandon hope
as a Vergilian trait. Lyne's study of pathos includes A. 4.494-7, 646-53; and
12.45 Iff, 473ff. Muecke considers the peculiar force of Dido's gestures of rejection.
Newbold's study of sensitivity to shame samples the first 250 verses from all the
epic books. Perotti's Latin article includes the slaughter of Priam, Dido and Anna,
Dido and Cleopatra, and Aeneas and Lavinia in its assessment of new directions.
Putnam's article brings Cacus, Pallas and Dido into fresh focus as he interprets the
final scene of the epic. The conclusion is a demonstration of the epic's force: "the
last lines of the poem look not to any new-found sense of order which might have
been reached from an ethical use of arms leading to reestablishment of order but to
the life of Aeneas' final victim suffering the ultimate indignity of death." (19).
Charles Segal's article offers a steady, sensible discussion of the elements of sound
criticism of the classics. Suerbaum deals with the motif of divine guidance for
Aeneas in the pre-Vergilian and Vergilian tradition. Taliercio studies Callimachean
aetiology and Vergilian practice in the accounts of Alba Longa, Misenum, Palinurus,
and Caieta. Thomas (94) offers bright new insights into the persons and experiences
involved in Aeneas' quest and recalls Brooks Otis' dictum ( Virgil : A Study in Civilized Poetry , [Oxford 1963] 385) that Aeneas as hero "overcomes Dido and Turnus
inside himself." Vicenzi's review of Carthage and its recurrences in the epic
highlights fides Punica. Deryck Williams' offering prefaces each book with a survey
of subject matter and treatment, and with characteristic care and insight, provides a
general discussion of the epic's literary qualities in an Introduction. A glossary, and
four appendixes (all models of fuller literary appreciation) complete this memorable
guide for the general reader.

F. Stok, " Iapyx Iasides (Verg. Aen. 12, 391-392)," BRINDISI 191-8.
Manuwald responds to Greek dramatic features in the Sinon episode with
attention to the speeches, the role of Calchas, and characterization. Ronconi's
exegesis relates to A. 2.13-20. Ferraro deals with the problematic nature of nostrae
iniuria caedis and favors caesum armentum over consilium caedis as both more
concrete and appropriate. Doubts and anxiety are, according to Giammarco, the
hallmark of Aeneid 3, aptly reflected in language and style. Harrison notes that
Achaemenides is silent about the final manoeuver, his own desertion by Odysseus.
The technical language of seafaring occupies Zurli. Morwood is concerned with the
context of the Atlas episode: Mount Atlas is, in his view, designed to embody a
symbol of endurance, an apt exemplum for Aeneas, a paradigm of qualities

conspicuously absent in Aeneas, toughness and a readiness to subordinate ever
to his mission. The mountain simile at A. 12.701-3, signals that Aeneas is now
imbued with the stamina characteristic of Vergil's Italians. Cowherd interprets A.
4.474-98 and discards notions of a witch priestess, a magical repertoire or love
magic; we are left with the curses and the efficacious death of the Punic queen.
Lippi prefers the reading magaria. Brugnoli points out that Vergil knows a larger
number of Alban kings than are cited in Aeneid 6. Fortuny associates aeripedem
with "wind-swift" movement. Perrone aligns inanes with ventos. Lassandro reconciles A. 7.601-23 with Livy, 1,19.1-4. The shock, associated with the Gates of War
and Juno's intervention, foreshadows the terrible intensity of the forthcoming
hostilities. Griffith examines the Hellenistic manner of literary allusion in the
simile-pair which describes the death of Euryalus. Rabel studies the thematic development of the Harpies episode and its immediate influence on the course of the
epic. Elements of the episode recur and associate Aeneas' Odyssey with the developing relations with Dido.

Desiderio examines common traits in Andromache, Creusa, and D
argues that Anchises is the true hero of the poem at the expense of his errant,
unstable son. Griffin is convinced that it is superficial to regard Aeneas as antiimperialist or anti-Augustan. The shield, his divinely-crafted burden, is a balance
between the achievement of Empire and the inevitable human cost. Griffith notes
that Aeneas is faceless and indistinct and that discrepancies and omissions are characteristic of his person. King argues that Vergil's resort to Achilles for his depiction
of Turnus (and sometimes Aeneas) signals his distaste for Homeric heroism, for its
irrational character and its threat to peace and civilization. Quetglas reconciles two
aspects of Aeneas, his role as representative of a lost cause, and his role as
triumphant forebear of Rome. Regner's excellent dissertation (supervised by von
Albrecht) offers a legalistic inquiry into the right and wrong of the Aeneas-Turnus
enmity. She champions adherence to the ethical systems of Vergil's Rome over the
historical and contemporary prejudices of some modern critics. Aeneas, in her view,
acts without blame in the final scene. Sachs argues that Aeneas' rage (12.946-7)
invalidates his potential as paradigm for Roman impersonal leadership, and that
self-discipline is a sine qua non for governing others. Skulsky regards Vergil's
reference to Catullus 66.39 and its Callimachean antecedent as "a brilliant
demonstration of intentional, complex, and controlled ambivalence, essent
the dramatic effect of the scene and to its thematic import" (p. 455). Ti
Dido's tragic hybris as her passionate behavior, forgetful of her queenly
responsibilities and of her oath to Sychaeus, and impervious to Aeneas' need to
leave Carthage. West detects important faults in both Chloreus and Camilla, and in
both Trojans and Latins. The effeminately dressed eunuch, and the evocations of a
sensuous, luxurious way of life, embarrasses the cause he ineffectually champions
and his death is a catharsis of Trojan weakness before Rome's advent. Wlosok's
study of heroism in Aeneas' nature is directional and provocative.

Lexicon Iconographicum Mythologiae Classicae II. 1: Aphrodisias- Athena
(Zurich, München 1984). Includes Aphrodite/ Venus; Apollo; Arethusa; Aristaeus;
Ascanius; Athena/Minerva, Ares/Mars, Artemis/Diana.
Colonna perceives the myth of Etruscan Dardanus as a product of north-east
Etruria, shaped by Greek culture which concentrated in Cortona. Grafton and
Swedlow attempt to recover the calendar date of Troy's fall from learned traditions.
They settle on a date near the beginning of summer. The Iconographie Lexicon
offers useful, concise accounts of figures in the Aeneid narrative, along with helpful
bibliographies and a compendium of illustrations.

raw_text:
Andrew Wallace-Hadrill, "The Golden Age and Sin in Augustan
Ideology," Past & Present 95 (1982) 19-36.
Religion and philosophy tend to be somewhat undernourished in Vergilian
scholarship. Some new advances and prospects have emerged in studies by Bianco,
Glücklich, and Quiter. Orphism and Pythagorean doctrine and practice claim their
due. Della Corte and Fasciano are concerned with Destiny, with Juno as protector of
Greece and Carthage, and with Venus as Aeneadum genetrix. Quiter's concern with
ritual aspects of Aeneas' descent excites fresh interest in the initiatory pattern of the
catabasis. Quiter favors the tunnel at Baiae over the Cumaean grotto as inspirational
source for Vergil, but the date is contested and the use obscure.

John Van Sickle, "Homer, Apollonius, Theocritus, Virgil," Atti del
Convegno Mondiale Scientifico di Studi Virgiliani 1 (Milan 1984) 124-47.
Avilés traces Catullian influence on Vergil (structure, vocabulary, and
metrics). Bettini treats A. 6.703 and Homer, Od. 24. Iff. Landolfi explores coincidences and contrasts in Vergil and Lucretius. Marastoni marshals Vergil's precursors and contemporaries in agricultural writings. Sanvitale matches vocabulary usage
in Catullus 64 and Aen. 4. Thornton observes that Vergil compares Dido with Venus,
not Diana. Nausicaa (likened to Artemis) is a passing sexual arousal for Odysseus,
whereas Dido is the object of a torrid passion, more suitable to the realm of Venus.
Van Sickle's essay will be reviewed with the remainder of the volume next year.
Wright regards Eclogue 1 as preface to the book, with programmatic intentions: to
provide a distinctively Roman pastoral which derived from Theocritus and
Callimachean principles.

VERGILIAN BIBLIOGRAPHY 1985-86
Ausonius and the translation of Christian values into the terms of Vergilian
So Paulinus tried to help Ausonius understand and accept his conversion. Robin
indicates that Filelfo's Odes 2, 2 is modelled on Vergil's underworld and reverberates with echoes of Aeneid 6; Odes 3, 4 is Vergilian only in the proemium; elsewhere the world of epic yields to Horatian satire. Scognamiglio's article surveys the
impact of Vergil on 19th century Germany, England, France, and Italy. Longfellow's conception of Nova Scotia as a New Troy is a token of the influence and
eternity of Vergil. C. P. Segal's essay on Classics and Comparative Literature is
required reading for any programmer or exponent. Shankman provides a critique of
an anti-pastoral pastoralist who adopts details and precedents from Vergil. Suerbaum
responds favorably to the cycle of illustrations provided by Barry Moser to the
second edition of Mandelbaum's translation of the epic. Verneťs concise account of
Vergil, from the 6th to the 15th century, explores the posthumous life of the poet,
anthologies, imitations of the Eclogues , Georgics, and Aeneid and the high reputation
of the poet's genius. Vidal provides an autopsy of the Vergilian cento which merges
Christian pastoral with doctrinal and theological matters. Wiltshire studies affinities
between the Kentucky poet and the Mantuan, and points to Vergil's influence on
Allen Tate's three major poems: "The Mediterranean," "Aeneas at Washington," and
"Aeneas at New York," and on his only novel, The Fathers.
